text: Firstly, the existing energy systems of SSA are already dominated by hydropower, which accounts for two-thirds of the total installed capacity and an even larger share of electricity produced (IRENA, 2015b). The remaining fossil-fired capacity consists mostly of gas- and oil-fired plants, but also includes significant amounts of coal power (especially in South Africa). Even though the hydropower capacity in SSA has been growing, its expansion has been limited by hydrological constraints and the fact that many of the best sites are already being exploited. The hydropower potential of SSA is estimated to be 500 GW (Baker et al., 2009), while the total installed capacity is now around 100 GW (IRENA, 2015b); thus, most of the economically viable hydropower potential has already been tapped. Secondly, solar photovoltaic (PV) and wind power have grown tremendously in recent years, and they are increasingly seen as the main source of future low-carbon electricity generation (REN21, 2015; REN21, 2016). However, PV and wind are inherently intermittent sources of electricity, which makes them difficult to integrate into the weak electricity grids of SSA. Thirdly, there are other zero-emission technologies available, such as nuclear and geothermal power. Nuclear power has the advantage of being dispatchable and highly scalable, but it suffers from extremely high investment costs and long lead times for construction, making it unattractive for developing regions (e.g. MIT, 2015). Geothermal power is generally quite affordable, and it offers a great potential for the East African Rift Valley region (Kamau et al., 2017; van der Hoeven, 2012), but it cannot alone provide enough power for the whole continent. text:
The obliquity of the ecliptic is the angle between the plane of the Earth's orbit around the sun and the plane of the Earth's equator. The Earth's orbit around the sun is not a perfect circle, but rather an ellipse, and the Earth's axis is tilted relative to its orbit. This tilt is what causes the seasons on Earth.

Imagine a toy top spinning on a table. The top's axis is tilted, and as it spins, it traces out a circle on the table. The angle between the top's axis and the table is the obliquity of the top. Similarly, the obliquity of the ecliptic is the angle between the Earth's axis and the plane of its orbit around the sun.

The obliquity of the ecliptic changes over time due to the gravitational influence of other planets in the solar system. Currently, the obliquity of the ecliptic is about 23.5 degrees, but it can vary between 22.1 and 24.5 degrees. This small change in the obliquity of the ecliptic has a big impact on the Earth's climate, as it determines the intensity of sunlight that reaches different parts of the Earth during different seasons.

The chance of new blood types emerging is very low, because it would require a significant change in the genes that control the production of the A and B antigens. This type of change is rare and would need to be passed down through many generations in order for a new blood type to become established in the population.

text:
Platt scaling, also known as Platt's probability calibration, is a method used to improve the predictive probability of a binary classifier. It is often used when the predicted probabilities of a classifier are poorly calibrated, meaning that the predicted probabilities do not accurately reflect the true probabilities of the predicted classes.



To apply Platt scaling, you need to have a set of predicted probabilities for a binary classification task and the corresponding true labels. You can then fit a sigmoid curve to these predicted probabilities using a maximum likelihood optimization procedure. The sigmoid curve maps the predicted probabilities to a new scale that is better calibrated with the true probabilities.



For example, suppose you have a classifier that predicts the probability of an email being spam. Without Platt scaling, the classifier might output probabilities that are too high or too low, leading to poor performance in terms of precision and recall. By applying Platt scaling, you can adjust the predicted probabilities to be more accurately calibrated with the true labels, resulting in improved performance on the classification task.



Platt scaling is a simple and effective way to improve the predictive probability of a binary classifier, and it is widely used in various applications, such as spam filtering and credit risk assessment.

text: And, perhaps most strikingly for Brazilians accustomed to skimpy beachwear, evangelicals are increasingly making their mark in fashion. Evangelical fashion accounts for less than 1 per cent of Brazil's textile market, which is dominated by European-style bikinis and thongs, said Antonio Carlos Ferreira, an economics professor at the University of Brasilia and author of several books on religion and politics. But he predicts that as the evangelical population continues to grow, they will soon account for between 10 and 15 percent of the $52 billion-a-year industry. 'The evangelical movement has gained power and influence in society, and that has translated into a bigger consumer base,' Ferreira said. One key to the growth of evangelical fashion is the increasing availability of mass-produced garments. Until recently, many women were forced to sew their own clothes because there was no other option, said Leila Farias, a 37-year-old evangelical mother of three from Sao Goncalo, another Rio de Janeiro suburb. 'I used to spend my Sundays sewing clothes,' she said. 'Now I don't need to do that anymore.' Some companies have begun to cater specifically to the evangelical market, such as Kauly, a small firm based in the southern state of Santa Catarina. Founded in 1997, Kauly produces everything from long-sleeve shirts to ponchos and capes, sold under brands such as Missa do Coracao (Mass of the Heart) and Meu Testamento (My Testament). Its products are available online and in some boutiques around the country. Kauly's business grew rapidly after founder Fernando Bittencourt appeared on a popular religious talk show four years ago and began promoting his line, said his wife, Denise Bittencourt. 'People started calling us, asking about our clothes,' she said. Sales rose from 300,000 reais ($164,500) in 2008 to 2 million reais last year, she added. For some, however, the increase in availability of evangelical fashion poses a dilemma. Evangeline dos Santos, a 31-year-old housewife from Sao Goncalo, said she loved her new long, flowing skirt and loose top - until her husband saw her wearing them. 'He said, "You can't go out dressed like that,"' she recalled. Dos Santos, who wears headscarves and long skirts to church services, said she is trying to strike a balance between her faith and her desire to look fashionable. 'I'm not going to stop dressing like this, but I want to find something different,' she said. text: The Supremacy Clause is a provision in the United States Constitution that establishes that the federal Constitution, and federal law generally, take precedence over state laws, and even state constitutions. This means that if there is a conflict between federal and state laws, the federal law will be supreme and will be applied. However, the Supremacy Clause does not mean that federal laws can override the Constitution itself. The Constitution is the supreme law of the land, and it establishes the powers of the federal government and limits on those powers. Congress, as one of the branches of the federal government, does have the power to pass laws, but those laws must be consistent with the Constitution. If a law passed by Congress is found to be in conflict with the Constitution, it can be challenged in court and potentially be struck down as unconstitutional. So, in summary, a law passed by Congress can't directly override the Constitution, but it can be found to be in conflict with the Constitution and be struck down by the courts. The only way to change the Constitution itself is through the amendment process, which requires the approval of two-thirds of both houses of Congress or a convention called for by two-thirds of the states, and ratification by three-fourths of the states.

text:
PGP (Pretty Good Privacy) is a way to encrypt and decrypt messages so that they can be sent securely over the internet. It works by using two keys: a public key and a private key. 
The public key is used to encrypt a message. It is called a "public" key because you can give it to anyone who wants to send you a message. When someone wants to send you a message, they use your public key to scramble it so that no one else can read it. 
The private key is used to decrypt the message. It is called a "private" key because you should keep it a secret and not share it with anyone. Only you should have access to your private key. When you receive an encrypted message, you use your private key to unscramble it and read the message. 
This is how PGP keeps your messages safe: even if someone else intercepts an encrypted message, they won't be able to read it unless they have your private key. Since you are the only one who should have your private key, only you will be able to read the message. 
So to summarize, PGP works by using two keys to encrypt and decrypt messages. It is considered safe because only the person who has the private key can read the message, even if someone else intercepts the encrypted message.

text: A similar mechanism may also be involved in within-person face recognition, whereby exposure to multiple views of a single face leads to the creation of an internal representation of the average of those views. Such a model could account for the fact that children's ability to generalize between different views of the same face is limited initially (Ellis, 1992; Mondloch et al., 2003; Sporer et al., 2007), but gradually develops with age (Mondloch et al., 2003). Children younger than 8 years have difficulty recognizing familiarized faces when they differ in viewpoint (Mondloch et al., 2003; Sporer et al., 2007), whereas older children and adolescents show good generalization across viewpoints (Mondloch et al., 2003). There is some evidence that infants also form representations of individual faces (Cohen & Strauss, 1979; Fagan, 1979), but these representations appear to be view specific, with infants failing to recognize the same face in a new orientation (Fagan, 1979). Children's Ability to Generalize Between Different Views of the Same Face In this article, we report three experiments investigating whether there is a developmental improvement in within-person face recognition. Experiment 1 investigated whether children aged 10-11 years, who are known to perform similarly to adults on measures of between-person face recognition (Carey et al., 1980; Mondloch et al., 2003; Susilo et al., 2013), would display any improvement over adults in their ability to group together naturally varying images of the same identity. Experiments 2 and 3 then assessed the ability of younger children, aged 5-6 and 7-8 years, respectively, to recognize the same face across multiple natural variations. All procedures were approved by the University of Western Australia Human Research Ethics Committee. Participants provided informed written consent before taking part in the experiment. A total of 42 participants took part in Experiment 1: 21 undergraduate students (mean age = 19.81 years, SD = 4.02 years) recruited via the School of Psychology Participant Pool at the University of Western Australia and 21 primary schoolchildren (mean age = 10.79 years, SD = .69 years) recruited from local schools. One additional child was tested but excluded due to failure to comply with instructions. All participants had normal or corrected-to-normal vision and no history of neurological impairment or learning disability. Stimuli consisted of the same set of 40 photographs used by Jenkins et al. (2011), comprising 20 images each of two male Dutch celebrities. Photographs were sourced from the Internet using Google Images search engine, and all images depicted front-facing views of the head and shoulders. Photographs varied in terms of background, lighting, focus, resolution, contrast, and color saturation. Most importantly, the images also varied naturally in terms of facial pose, gaze direction, hairstyle, and facial expression (see Figure 1). Faces were cropped out of the original photographs and resized to 450 * 600 pixels. Stimuli were presented on a standard laptop computer using E-Prime software. Figure 1. Example stimuli depicting natural variation in real-life photographs of the same identity.
